has been said about them. Compare: talked about. This is preferred when we  'bring things into focus' before anything It is only used to refer to things which  are 'in focus' - which  have already been 3 focus This was badly damaged. (The table was damaged.) I was carrying the statue to my office when I dropped it on the kitchen table.

It was badly damaged. (The statue was damaged.) - I was carrying the statue to my office when I dropped it on the kitchen table.

by the children, incidentally. (The spare room is used by the children.) We keep the ice-cream machine in the spare room. This/That is mainly used children, incidentally. (The machine is used by the children.) - We keep the ice-cream machine in the spare room. It is mainly used by the introduced (often the last thing mentioned). Compare: subject of discussion; this and that generally refer to a new subject that has been When more than one thing has been mentioned, it generally refers to the main 2 more    than    one   thing the;   my,    your,          this,    that,           Section12 Determiners:       a/an   and           etc;        etc grammar •  Section 13   Determiners: Quantifiers the fewest problems - these few poems the least difficulty - a little time the most money - his many friends in some cases.

Many, most, little, least, few and fewest can follow 'group 1' determiners directly Much  of philosophy is concerned with questions that have no answers.

Most of Wales was without electricity last night.

uncountable nouns that refer to the whole of a subject or activity.

happens with proper nouns such as place names, and sometimes with A  quantifier with of can be used directly before a noun in a few cases. This most of them most horses The  same thing happens before a pronoun. Compare: neither of these doors - neither door each of my children - each child some of the people - some people or demonstrative, ►    Section 12), we  use a structure with of. Compare: When  a quantifier is put together with a 'group 1' determiner (article, possessive Have you got any more coffee?

We meet every few days.

Quantifiers can be put together if the combination makes sense.

putting      determiners         together:      use   of  of only, such, which, what and numbers) are covered elsewhere - see the Index.

A  few words which  can have determiner and non-determiner uses (e.g. other, here, see the Index.

convenience, along with some other uses of these words. For uses not included This ‘ pronoun' use of quantifiers is dealt with here, and not in Section 14, for Fish? He eats some, but not much.

He doesn't eat any meat, and not much  fish.

used without following nouns if the meaning is clear. Compare: Besides their use as determiners in noun phrases, these words can generally be other     uses class or category we  are talking about (all, most, some, none,.. .).
